app.heroSubtitle: EV vs ICEV: Cost, Industry, Policy, Environment — A Four-Dimensional Data-Driven Interactive Exploration
app.act1Subtitle: Crunch the numbers on EV vs ICEV from a Total Cost of Ownership perspective
app.act2Title: How Are Batteries and R&D Reshaping the Industry?
app.act2Subtitle: Battery cost decline, market competition landscape, and China market structural shifts
app.act3Title: Who Is Driving, Who Is Holding Back?
app.act3Subtitle: Global policy intensity, market penetration, and infrastructure dynamics
app.act4Title: Under the Blue Sky, Which Is Cleaner?
app.act4Subtitle: Full lifecycle carbon emission comparison — from battery manufacturing to on-road driving
app.footerText: An interactive data documentation project on global electric vehicles vs ICE vehicles. Through a four-act structure, it explores the truth of electrification from four dimensions: economics, industry, policy, and environment.
app.footerMethod1: TCO Model: Annual cumulative depreciation + city-weighted energy costs
app.footerMethod3: Policy Sandbox: OLS multiple linear regression (R²=0.65, n=400)
app.footerMethod4: Carbon Race: Woody 2026 full lifecycle parameters
app.footerBottom: Built with Vue 3 + ECharts + D3.js | Data as of April 2026
app.endBody1: Electrification is not a simple "good vs bad" story. It hinges on whether the economics work out, whether batteries break through cost floors, whether policies strike the right balance, and whether grids can keep pace.
app.endBody2: The data shows us the direction, but every country and every individual is choosing their own path.
app.endResearchQuestion: This visualization addresses one central question: across four dimensions — cost, industry, policy, and environment — have electric vehicles truly surpassed internal combustion vehicles?
app.endResearchDesign: Design Decisions: A four-act narrative structure, each act focusing on one dimension. The TCO model (exponential depreciation) reveals long-term economics; battery price curves (exponential decay fit, R²=0.96) illustrate cost trends; the policy sandbox (OLS regression, R²=0.65, n=400) enables interactive exploration of policy levers; the carbon race chart (lifecycle parameters) compares environmental costs. Interaction design follows the principle of "let users discover the data themselves" — all charts support hover, filtering, and parameter adjustment.
app.endResearchSources: Data Sources: IEA Global EV Data Explorer 2025 · BloombergNEF Battery Price Survey · Washington State EV Registration Data · World Bank Open Data · Woody et al. 2026 (Environ. Res. Lett.) · Noll et al. 2026 (Nature Energy) · Baek et al. 2026 (eTransportation)

scenario.emptyHint: Use the "Save" button in any act to add scenarios for comparison.

act1.title: Act 1: Total Cost of Ownership Data Sources
act1.sources: IEA Global EV Data Explorer 2025 — fuel/electricity price references||Washington State EV Registration Data — actual registrations validation||World Bank Open Data — GDP per capita, urbanization rates||Consumer Reports & Kelley Blue Book — vehicle MSRP and depreciation rates
act1.methodology: Depreciation model: exponential decay (1-r)^t, where r is annual depreciation rate (ICEV 10-12%, BEV 13-15%). Annual costs are age-adjusted: insurance decreases 3%/year, maintenance increases 5%/year. Energy cost based on 15,000 km/year baseline with mileage scaling. Charging strategy affects BEV/PHEV energy cost via home/public mix ratio.
act1.limitations: MSRP and annual costs are US market representative values, not model-specific. Depreciation rates are industry averages — actual depreciation varies significantly by brand and region. Does not include residual value subsidies or local tax incentives.

act2.title: Act 2: Battery & Industry Data Sources
act2.sources: BloombergNEF Annual Battery Price Survey — 2010-2024 pack prices||IEA Global EV Outlook 2025 — global EV sales and market share||Woody et al. 2026 (Environ. Res. Lett.) — battery manufacturing emissions||China Passenger Car Association — China EV market structure by segment
act2.methodology: Battery price prediction: exponential decay fit on 2010-2024 actual data (price = a·e^(-bt)), R²=0.96. Brand competition data from global-ev-2026-dataset.csv. China market structure shows annual sales share by vehicle class (A00-C).
act2.limitations: Battery price prediction assumes constant decay rate, which may be disrupted by raw material price volatility. 2022 data showed a temporary rebound due to lithium price spike. Brand competition data is synthetic/aggregated, not exact brand-level sales.

act3.title: Act 3: Policy & Market Penetration Data Sources
act3.sources: IEA Global EV Data Explorer 2025 — 25-country EV sales/market share/charging stations/subsidies||World Bank Open Data — GDP per capita, urbanization rates||Emisia/ICCT emission regulation database — regulation scores
act3.methodology: OLS multiple linear regression: dependent variable is EV market share, independent variables are subsidy amount, log(charging stations+1), fuel price, electricity price, regulation score, log(GDP+1). Log transforms handle skewed distributions. Sensitivity analysis measures partial R² drop from removing each variable.
act3.limitations: Linear regression assumes linear relationships, potentially underestimating extreme policies (e.g., Norway). Market share has [0, 100] bounds, model may predict out-of-range values. Some 2024-2025 data points are extrapolated/estimated.

act4.title: Act 4: Lifecycle Carbon Emissions Data Sources
act4.sources: Woody et al. 2026 (Environ. Res. Lett. 21 024022) — lifecycle emission parameters||IEA Electricity Information 2025 — country grid CO₂ intensity||IPCC AR6 — fuel emission factor (2.31 kg CO₂/L gasoline)
act4.methodology: BEV annual emissions = vehicle energy consumption × grid CO₂ intensity. ICEV annual emissions = fuel consumption × fuel emission factor. Manufacturing emissions include body + battery manufacturing (extra for BEV). Crossover year calculated via linear interpolation. Grid CO₂ linearly scales BEV annual emissions relative to US baseline (0.39 kg/kWh).
act4.limitations: Manufacturing emission data are literature averages, not specific to manufacturers or battery chemistry. Grid CO₂ uses national averages, ignoring regional/time variation. Does not include carbon credits from battery recycling or second-life applications. 15-year ownership is an assumed upper bound.

act1.insightTitle: A Used EV Is the True Cost Champion
act1.insightBody: Woody et al. 2026 conducted the first comparative TCO analysis of used vehicles (70% of all U.S. car purchases). Buying a 3-year-old used Midsize SUV BEV saves approximately $13,000 over a 7-year ownership period vs a new ICEV of the same class. The paper notes: "BEVs depreciate more rapidly than other powertrains in the first several years of vehicle life" — precisely this accelerated depreciation makes used BEVs the best value.
act1.insightDetail: Use the TCO calculator below to verify this conclusion
act1.narrativeP1: Most people only look at the sticker price when buying a car. But Total Cost of Ownership (TCO) is the real basis for decision-making — it includes depreciation, energy, maintenance, insurance, taxes, and other cumulative expenses across multiple dimensions. Baek et al. 2026 confirms that for new vehicles, ICEVs generally have lower TCO, but this gap is closing — BEV300 is projected to deliver the lowest TCO by 2050.
act1.narrativeP2: Although EVs may cost more at purchase, their energy costs are far lower than ICEVs. When you extend the timeline to 5–7 years, the cumulative savings from electricity become very significant. Notably, Noll et al. 2026 found that even in infrastructure-poor Africa, BEVs with solar off-grid charging will achieve cost advantages before 2040 — "Financing is identified as the key action point." Use the calculator below to select different powertrains and see how their cost curves intersect.

chart.breakevenCheaperFromStart: {label} is cheaper from the start — no payback period needed
chart.savingsDetail: {label} saves more over the ownership period
chart.savingsDetailMulti: {cheapest} savings over {mostExpensive} across the ownership period

act2.insightTitle: Battery Costs Dropped 90% in 15 Years, But This Is Only the Beginning
act2.insightBody: From $1,160/kWh in 2010 to approximately $83/kWh in 2025, battery costs have declined exponentially. Projected to reach $26/kWh by 2030, at which point batteries will no longer be the cost bottleneck for EVs.
act2.narrativeP1: The battery is the single most expensive component in an EV, accounting for 30–40% of the vehicle cost. The trajectory of battery cost changes directly determines when EVs will achieve purchase price parity with ICEVs. Noll et al. (2026) corroborate this in Nature Energy: BEV cost advantages are accelerating globally — "battery electric vehicles with solar off-grid chargers will have lower costs well before 2040 in most countries and segments."
act2.narrativeP2: The changes in the Chinese market are particularly striking: once dominated by low-cost micro-cars (A00 class), the market has now shifted toward mid-to-high-end mainstream models (A-class and B-class), reflecting the transition of EVs from "basic commuting tools" to "quality mobility."
act2.narrativeP3: The cliff-like drop in battery costs has completely rewritten the product logic of one market — China. In 2016, A00 micro-cars held half the market; by 2023, A-class and B-class vehicles had become the absolute mainstream. Baek et al. 2026 notes that "modern ICEVs generally have lower TCO; however, BEVs and FCHEVs could match or have lower TCOs than ICEVs over time" — this isn't consumption upgrade, it's cost structure allowing EVs to enter the mainstream price band.
act2.batteryChartDesc: 2010–2030: actual prices vs exponential decay prediction (R²=0.96)
act2.chinaChartDesc: Vehicle class share evolution, 2016–2023 (click to explore)
act2.brandChartDesc: 10 brands across 10 countries — bubble size = battery capacity

act3.insightTitle: Policy Is Not Just Icing on the Cake — It's a Critical Driver
act3.insightBody: Regression analysis shows that the number of charging stations, fuel prices, and regulatory strength are the strongest predictors of EV penetration. When charging infrastructure grows from zero to China's 4.3 million chargers in 2025, the model predicts market penetration can increase by 20+ percentage points. Woody et al. 2026 also show that charging strategy significantly impacts BEV energy costs, further validating the critical role of infrastructure.
act3.narrativeP1: Data from 25 countries over 16 years tells us: there is no single policy "silver bullet" for electrification. Norway leads with hefty tax exemptions, China overtakes with a dual subsidy + infrastructure drive, while Brazil and India are still waiting for the price inflection point. Noll et al. 2026 warns from the African context: "Financing is identified as the key action point for governments" — financing, not subsidies per se, is the core accelerator.
act3.narrativeP2: The sandbox below lets you play "policy maker": adjust 6 parameters including subsidies, fuel prices, electricity prices, and regulatory strength, and see how the regression model predicts changes in EV market penetration.
act3.narrativeP3: But policy is not omnipotent. In the regression model, the marginal effect of subsidies diminishes significantly after penetration exceeds 15% — at which point infrastructure and electricity prices become the real bottlenecks. Baek et al. 2026 similarly notes that "further investigation is warranted for BEV charging infrastructure" — the completeness of charging infrastructure directly determines whether policy dividends can be realized.
act3.heatmapChartDesc: 25 countries ranked by EV market share — click a bar to see country details
act3.sandboxChartDesc: Adjust 6 policy parameters — see predicted EV market share in real-time
act3.sensitivityChartDesc: Which factor has the largest impact on EV adoption?

act4.insightTitle: An EV's "Carbon Debt" Can Be Repaid in 2–3 Years
act4.insightBody: Woody et al. 2026 shows that although battery manufacturing generates additional carbon emissions (~5–6.5 tonnes CO₂), BEVs catch up to ICEVs within 2–3 years thanks to lower annual operational emissions. Their cross-analysis across 5 vehicle classes and 17 U.S. cities confirms: "the finding that BEVs have the lowest total cost for used vehicles is robust across these variables." In clean-grid regions (e.g., Norway, France), this advantage is even more pronounced.
act4.narrativeP1: "But don't EVs also use electricity? Where does the electricity come from?" This is a critical question. Noll et al. 2026 conducted lifecycle analysis across 52 African countries, showing that even in high grid-carbon-intensity regions, BEVs with solar off-grid charging achieve "negative greenhouse gas abatement costs." Full lifecycle carbon emission analysis shows that even in coal-dominated countries like China and India, EVs still produce fewer emissions than comparable ICEVs — the magnitude of the advantage just differs.
act4.narrativeP2: In Norway (98% clean electricity), the EV carbon advantage reaches 96.6%; in India (25% clean electricity), this figure drops to 18.2%. Baek et al. 2026's technology progress scenario analysis also identifies grid decarbonization as the core variable amplifying BEV carbon advantages. Grid cleanliness directly determines the environmental "return on investment" of driving an EV.
act4.carbonRaceDesc: Cumulative lifecycle CO₂ emissions — watch the crossover point
act4.gridChartDesc: EV carbon advantage by country — driven by grid CO₂ intensity
act4.emissionChartDesc: Million liters of gasoline equivalent saved, by region (2010–2024)
